title: FSW and SWU
subtitle: Plain-text encodings, character sets, and conversion
summary: Plain-text encodings, explicit character inventories, conversion, and canonical text boundaries.
synopsis: Formal SignWriting depends on clear plain-text encodings. This entry explains FSW and SWU as two technical encodings of the same model, clarifies the usual canonical role of FSW in processing, makes the SWU inventory and numeric ranges explicit, and frames conversion as part of the encoding layer rather than the rendering layer.
cover_image_alt: Cover image for FSW and SWU in the Formal SignWriting series.

publication_plan.doi_strategy: publish as distinct citable artifacts connected by series identity and related identifiers
publication_plan.zenodo_record_title_suggestion: FSW and SWU: Plain-Text Encodings, Character Sets, and Conversion
publication_plan.citation_stub: Slevinski, S. (2026). FSW and SWU: Plain-Text Encodings, Character Sets, and Conversion. Formal SignWriting.

slides.title: Two Plain-Text Encodings, One Model
slides.summary: FSW and SWU are plain-text encodings of the same technical model, not two different theories of writing.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for FSW and SWU as two encodings of one model.

image_prompts.concrete: Technical comparison graphic showing one sign represented in FSW and SWU side by side, connected to one underlying model, white background, elegant software-standards design
image_prompts.abstract: Two text streams converging on one stable underlying structure

slides.speech: Before we get deeper into sign layout, grammar, or rendering, we need the encoding layer to be clear. FSW and SWU are technical encodings of Formal SignWriting text. They belong to the formal model. They are not rendering shortcuts and they are not presentation markup. In current stewardship practice, FSW is usually the canonical internal form, while SWU remains available for Unicode-oriented input, output, and interchange.
slides.notes: Settle the layer model early. Encodings belong to the architecture. Rendering and styling come later. Character strategy does not change the model.

slides.title: The Inventory Has Exact Ranges
slides.summary: SWU is not vague Unicode text. It defines exact ranges for markers, number characters, the null symbol, and the symbol inventory.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the explicit SWU character inventory.

image_prompts.concrete: Technical inventory slide showing SignWriting Unicode ranges for markers numbers null symbol and full symbol inventory, academic software style, white background
image_prompts.abstract: A structured character inventory divided into clear technical ranges

slides.speech: The inventory should be stated concretely. SWU uses exact Unicode ranges, not a loose idea of Unicode support. The structural markers occupy U plus 1D800 through 1D804. The dedicated number characters occupy U plus 1D80C through 1D9FF. The null symbol is U plus 40000. The symbol inventory runs from U plus 40001 through U plus 4F428. That precision matters because conversion and validation depend on an explicit inventory.
slides.notes: Emphasize exact ranges. Make clear that numbers are their own block. Explicit inventory is part of technical maturity.

slides.title: Symbol Conversion Is Computed
slides.summary: SWU symbol characters are derived systematically from base, fill, and rotation.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for computed symbol mapping between FSW and SWU.

image_prompts.concrete: Technical formula slide showing FSW symbol key parts mapping to an SWU code point, clean academic software diagram, white background
image_prompts.abstract: A structured mapping from symbolic parts into a computed encoded result

slides.speech: At the symbol level, conversion is computed rather than memorized. The FSW key keeps the three important parts visible: base, fill, and rotation. SWU preserves the same distinctions, but carries them as a code point on Plane 4. The usual way to explain it is in two steps. First compute the symbol ID from base, fill, and rotation. Then add that ID to U plus 40000. So S10000 produces ID 1 and maps to U plus 40001.
slides.notes: Do not over-teach hexadecimal arithmetic. The main point is that the mapping is systematic. S10000 to U+40001 is the anchor example.

slides.title: Coordinates Share One Number Space
slides.summary: FSW and SWU use the same bounded numeric space for coordinates, even though the characters differ.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for shared coordinate logic in FSW and SWU.

image_prompts.concrete: Technical coordinate slide showing one FSW coordinate string and two SWU number characters representing the same values, clean academic layout
image_prompts.abstract: One numeric space expressed through two different textual surfaces

slides.speech: The coordinate model is shared. Valid values run from 250 to 749, giving a bounded space of 500 numbers. FSW writes those values with decimal digits, such as 525 x 535. SWU writes the same values with dedicated number characters from U plus 1D80C through U plus 1D9FF. There is no literal x separator in SWU. The coordinate is simply carried as two consecutive number characters, one for x and one for y. That is a real encoding decision, not a rendering trick.
slides.notes: Remove hand-waviness around coordinates. Same numeric model, different character strategy, no literal x in SWU.

slides.title: Conversion Is a Promise of Interoperability
slides.summary: Conversion matters because the model remains legible across multiple textual forms.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for FSW and SWU conversion.

image_prompts.concrete: Technical interoperability slide showing FSW and SWU converting back and forth with one stable sign in the center, clean scholarly layout
image_prompts.abstract: Two textual forms circulating around one preserved sign identity

slides.speech: Conversion belongs naturally in this entry because it is part of the encoding story. The point is not only that conversion is possible. The point is that the model remains coherent across multiple textual forms. That continuity matters for storage, interchange, software generations, and technical maintenance.
slides.notes: Make conversion feel foundational rather than incidental.

slides.title: Where Canonical Text Ends
slides.summary: The style string is presentation markup, not part of canonical sign text.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for canonical text versus markup.
slides.speech: One of the central distinctions should be made early. FSW and SWU are core text encodings of the same model. In current stewardship practice, FSW is commonly the canonical internal form, while SWU remains available for Unicode-oriented interchange. The style string is optional light markup for presentation and customization. That distinction protects the technical architecture from being flattened into display concerns and keeps the model much clearer for implementers.
slides.notes: Use a markup analogy carefully. The goal is to clarify layers, not overstate equivalence with HTML.
